Swiss CitiesBülach

Bülach

ZHZurich

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The rune-writer's secret

A child discovers a glowing brooch in the earth near the church hill and is transported to 6th-century Buelach. There they meet the Alemannic woman who wore the Buelach fibula, learning the meaning of the mysterious runic inscription -- the only Elder Futhark runes ever found in Switzerland -- and discovering a name that has waited 1,400 years to be spoken aloud.

Create This Story

In 1927, the Buelach fibula was discovered in an Alemannic grave -- a silver disc-shaped brooch with almandine inlay dating to the 6th century (550-600 AD). It bears an Elder Futhark runic inscription, the only one found in Switzerland to date. The inscription contains a personal name demonstrating early Alemannic linguistic characteristics. The grave belonged to an adult woman, and the brooch demonstrates sophisticated metalworking. Buelach was first documented in 811 AD as 'Pulacha.'

The green glass jar that remembered

A vintage Buelach preserving jar on grandmother's shelf begins whispering memories: of the glassblowers in the factory, of wartime gardens when German jars could not be imported, of summer fruit picked in the Glatt Valley. Each memory is a chapter in Swiss home life, and the child learns that a simple green glass jar can hold more than cherries -- it holds a century of stories.

Create This Story

The Glashuette Buelach AG was founded in 1891. During World War I, when German Weck preserving jars could no longer be imported, the Swiss government encouraged a domestic alternative. In 1920, the iconic Buelach preserving jar came to market -- thick green glass with rubber ring and porcelain lid. By 1924 it was a national brand known in the most remote Swiss villages. Production ran from 1920 to 1978; the factory closed in February 2002 after 111 years. Today original jars are prized vintage collectibles.

The gridiron in the sky

On Saint Lawrence's feast day, the golden gridiron on Buelach's coat of arms lifts off the town hall and floats above the Altstadt. A child must follow it through the cobblestone streets, solving riddles at each landmark -- the castle, the city wall, the 74-metre church tower -- to return it to the coat of arms before dawn.

Create This Story

Buelach's coat of arms features the gridiron of Saint Lawrence, who was martyred in 258 AD in Rome by being roasted on a grid. Lawrence has been the patron saint of Buelach since at least 811 -- one of the longest-standing dedications in the region. The oldest town seal from 1424 shows the gridiron flanked by Austrian shields. The Reformed Church of Saint Laurentius has a tower rising to 74 metres, visible from far across the Zuercher Unterland.

Market day time-slip

At the Tuesday Bueli-Maert, a child passes through an old archway in the city wall and lands in 1384 -- the very first market day after Duke Leopold granted the town charter. They must help a young vendor sell all their wares before the market bell rings, discovering how 640 years of commerce connect the medieval charter to the bustling stalls of today.

Create This Story

In 1384, Duke Leopold III of Austria (Habsburg) granted Buelach a town charter including the right to hold markets -- the same rights as Winterthur. The traditional Bueli-Maert takes place on Tuesdays with 120-160 vendors. Three large annual markets continue the tradition dating back to the 1384 charter. Major city fires devastated Buelach in 1386, 1444, and 1506, each time requiring rebuilding.

The glass-blower's apprentice

Inside the Glashuette Buelach, a child apprentice learns to shape molten glass but accidentally traps a tiny flame spirit inside a green preserving jar. Together they must journey through the glowing factory -- past roaring furnaces and spinning machines -- to set the spirit free before the furnace cools and the glass hardens forever.

Create This Story

The Glashuette Buelach factory operated on a 42,000 square metre site from 1891 to 2002. In 1917 the first large American glass-blowing machine was installed. The characteristic green glass became a defining visual signature -- instantly recognisable in Swiss kitchens. After the factory closed, an urban design competition led to the Glasi-Quartier development by Duplex Architekten with around 560 apartments and 20,000 square metres of commercial space on the former factory site.

The woodpecker's trail

Following the rare Mittelspecht woodpecker through the Hardwald forest, a child discovers that each of the nine trail stations is guarded by a different forest creature. Each creature holds a piece of a forgotten local legend about how Buelach got its name -- and only the woodpecker, tapping on the oldest oak, knows the final answer.

Create This Story

The Hardwald forest near Buelach is home to the Mittelspecht (middle spotted woodpecker), a rare and endangered bird in Switzerland. A 2.3 km themed trail with nine stations teaches visitors about the bird and its old-oak habitat. The trail includes four 'forest windows' showing the forest at different stages of development. Buelach's area is 39.5% forest and 33.2% agricultural land, reflecting its position between the urban Zurich agglomeration and the rural Zuercher Unterland.

The tower that told time

The 74-metre church tower of Saint Laurentius begins chiming at unusual hours. A child climbs to the belfry and finds a clockwork map revealing hidden passages beneath the old town -- connecting the castle, the tithe barn, and the Sigristenkeller gallery. Each passage holds an echo from a different century, from the 650 AD noblewoman's grave to the great fire of 1506.

Create This Story

The Reformed Church of Saint Laurentius has a tower rising to 74 metres -- visible from far across the Zuercher Unterland. The church site has held a place of worship since at least 650 AD, with remnants of a noblewoman's grave beneath the foundations. After the city fire of 1506, the church was rebuilt in late Gothic style between 1508-1510. The Rathaus dates from 1672 and features an octagonal ceramic stove by potter Hans Heinrich Graf from 1673 with biblical motifs and council members' coats of arms.

Gottfried Keller's lost notebook

While visiting the Keller museum in nearby Glattfelden, a child finds a hidden notebook with an unfinished story. Following clues along the Dichterweg -- past the Heidenstube cave, through the fields Keller's parents once farmed -- and into Buelach's Altstadt, the child must write the ending, discovering that the best stories come from knowing the land beneath your feet.

Create This Story

Gottfried Keller (1819-1890), one of Switzerland's greatest writers, had deep roots in neighbouring Glattfelden -- both his parents came from there. The Zuercher Unterland landscape is woven into his masterpiece 'Der gruene Heinrich' (Green Henry). The Gottfried-Keller-Zentrum museum in Glattfelden (opened 1985) preserves his legacy. A Dichterweg (poets' path) with over 20 plaques leads hikers through the landscape that inspired his work, including the 'Heidenstube' cave on the Laubberg.
